A P ) ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-060.jpg?height=762&width=891&top_left_y=1788&top_left_x=322) | Algebraic Method | | :--- | | Consider the following system of equation
There are following three methods under Algebraic method to solve the above system:
(i) Substitution method
(a) Find the value of one variable, say y in terms of x or x in terms of y
(b) Substitute this value in second equation to get equation in one variable
(c) Now substitute the value/solution so obtained in the equation got
(ii) Elimination Method
Write equation as $a_{1} x+b_{1} y+c_{1}=0 ; a_{2} x+b_{2} y+c_{2}=0$ from one equation. and find solution. in step(a)
(a) If coefficient of one variable are not same in both the equation multiply both the equation with suitable non-zero constants to make coefficient of one variable numerically equal.
(b) Add or subtract the equation to get equation in one variable and solve it.
(c) Now substitute the value got in the above step in either of the original equation to get value of the other variable.
(iii) Cross multiplication method
Consider the following diagram.![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-060.jpg?height=468&width=125&top_left_y=904&top_left_x=974) $\frac{x}{b_{1} c_{2}-b_{2} c_{1}}=\frac{y}{c_{1} a_{2}-c_{2} a_{1}}=\frac{1}{a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1}}$
Solve it to get the solution provided, $a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1} \neq 0$ | | Graphical Method | | :--- | | Consider the standard form of linear equations in two variables. $a_{1} x+b_{1} y+c_{1}=0 ; a_{2} x+b_{2} y+c_{2}=0$
While solving the above system of equation by this method following three cases arise.
(i) If $\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}} \neq \frac{b_{1}}{b_{2}}$; system is called consistent, having one or unique solution and pair of straight lines representing the above equations intersect at one point only.
(ii) If $\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{b_{1}}{b_{2}}=\frac{c_{1}}{c_{2}}$; system is called dependent and have infinitely many solution. Pair of lines representing the equations coincide.
(iii) If $\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{b_{1}}{b_{2}} \neq \frac{c_{1}}{c_{2}}$; system is called inconsistent and has no solution. Pair of lines representing the equations are parallel or do not intersect at any point. | ## Equations Reducible to a Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables By using the suitable substitution or simplification first we convert the given system into the system of a pair of linear equations in two variables. Then after using any algebraic or graphical method we solve the system.

key_points.text: A symbol like $x$ in the above polynomial, which takes various numerical values is known as a variable.
In a polynomial, powers of the variable are non-negative integer.
Terms having same variable with same indices are called like terms.

Here, $a_{0}, a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots \ldots \ldots . ., a_{n}$ are real numbers. If $a_{0}, a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots \ldots \ldots ., a_{n}$ are all integers then the above polynomial is said to be a polynomial over integers.
If $a_{n} \neq 0$, then degree of the above polynomial is $n$.
$\quad 6 x^{7}-5 x^{4}+2 x+3$ is a polynomial of degree 7 .
$2+5 x^{3 / 2}+7 x^{2}$ is an expression but not a polynomial, since it contains a term in which power of $x$ is $3 / 2$, which is not a non-negative integer.
In $-9 y^{2},-9$ is the coefficient of $y ; y$ is the variable and 2 is the power or index of $y$.
$5 a^{2},-7 a^{2}$ and $\sqrt{2} a^{2}$ are like terms.
Monomial : A polynomial having one term is called a monomial. Example : 3, 5x, 6a etc.
Binomial : A polynomial having two terms is called a binomial. Example : $2 x^{2}+3,6 y-b, 3+7 z$ etc.
Trinomial : A polynomial having three terms is called a trinomial. Example : $30 x+b y+c z, 2 a-3 x^{2}+y^{3}$ etc.
Linear polynomials : A polynomial of degree one is called a linear polynomial. The general form of the linear polynomial is $a x+b$, where $a$ and $b$ are any real constant and $a \neq 0$.
Quadratic polynomials : A polynomial of degree two is called a quadratic polynomial. The general form of the quadratic polynomial is $a x^{2}+b x+c$, where $a \neq 0$.
Cubic polynomials : A polynomial of degree three is called a cubic polynomial. The general form of a cubic polynomial is $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$, where $a \neq 0$.

Biquadratic polynomials : A polynomial of degree four is called biquadratic polynomial. The general form of biquadratic polynomial is $a x^{4}+b x^{3}+c x^{2}+d x+e$, where $a \neq 0$. Example : $4 x^{4}-6 x^{3}+3 x^{2}$

case_studies.title: Case Study Based MCQs
case_studies.description:
1. Due to heavy storm an electric wire got bent as shown in the figure. It followed a mathematical shape. Answer the following questions below. ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-044.jpg?height=369&width=451&top_left_y=552&top_left_x=1192) [CBSE Sample Paper] (i) Name the shape in which the wire is bent (a) spiral (b) ellipse (c) linear (d) parabola (ii) How many zeroes are there for the polynomial (shape of the wire) (a) 2 (b) 3 (c) 1 (d) 0 (iii) The zeroes of the polynomial are (a) $-1,5$ (b) $-1,3$ (c) 3,5 (d) $-4,2$ (iv) What will be the expression of the polynomial? (a) $x^{2}+2 x-3$ (b) $x^{2}-2 x+3$ (c) $x^{2}-2 x-3$ (d) $x^{2}+2 x+3$ (v) What is the value of the polynomial if $\mathrm{x}=-1$ ? (a) 6 (b) -18 (c) 18 (d) 0 2. Soniya and Anuj are students of class $X$ and they given a polynomial such that "If one zero of the polynomial $3 x^{2}-8 x+2 k+5$ is four times the other $4 x^{2}-12 x+3 k+8$. Then, answer the following questions. (i) Find the sum of zeroes. (a) 3 (b) 4 (c) $\frac{12}{3}$ (d) $\frac{12}{5}$ (ii) For quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c, a \neq 0$, write the formula to find product of zeroes. (a) $\frac{b}{a}$ (b) $-\frac{b}{a}$ (c) $-\frac{c}{a}$ (d) $\frac{c}{a}$ (iii) If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the zeroes of given polynomial. Then, what is the relation between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ ? (a) $\alpha+\beta=4$ (b) $\alpha \beta=4$ (c) $\beta=4 \alpha$ (d) $\alpha^{2}=16 \beta$ (iv) If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the zeroes of the given polynomial, then find value of $\alpha$. (a) $\frac{1}{5}$ (b) $\frac{7}{4}$ (c) $\frac{2}{5}$ (d) $\frac{3}{5}$ (v) Find the value of $k$. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the zeroes of given polynomials. (a) $\frac{56}{75}$ (b) $-\frac{56}{75}$ (c) $\frac{75}{56}$ (d) $\frac{65}{75}$ ## Text-book Questions 1. The graphs of $y=p(x)$ are given in fig. below, for some polynomials $p(x)$. Find the number of zeroes of $p(x)$, in each case. (i) ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-045.jpg?height=276&width=319&top_left_y=762&top_left_x=374) (ii) ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-045.jpg?height=268&width=319&top_left_y=1032&top_left_x=374) 2. Find the zeroes of the following quadratic polynomials and verify the relationship between the zeroes and the coefficients. (i) $6 x^{2}-3-7 x$ (ii) $4 u^{2}+8 u$ 3. Find a quadratic polynomial each with the given numbers as the sum and product of its zeroes respectively. (i) $\sqrt{2}, \frac{1}{3}$ (ii) $-\frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{4}$ 4. Divide the polynomial $p(x)$ by the polynomial $g(x)$ and find the quotient and remainder in each of the following: (i) Divide $x^{3}-3 x^{2}+5 x-3$ by $x^{2}-2$ and verify the division algorithm. (ii) $p(x)=x^{4}-5 x+6, g(x)=2-x^{2}$ 5. Check whether the first polynomial is factor of the second polynomial by dividing the second polynomial by the first polynomial : $x^{3}-3 x+1, x^{5}-4 x^{3}+x^{2}+3 x+1$ 6. Obtain all other zeroes of $3 x^{4}+6 x^{3}-2 x^{2}-10 x-5$, if two of its zeroes are $\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ and $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$. 7. Give example of polynomials $p(x), g(x), q(x), r(x)$ which satisfy the division algorithm and (i) degree $p(x)=$ degree $q(x)$ (ii) degree $q(x)=\operatorname{deg} r(x)$ (iii) degree $r(x)=0$ ## Exemplar Questions 1. Find the zeroes of the polynomial by factorization and verify the relations between the zeroes and the coefficients of the polynomials: (a) $4 x^{2}+5 \sqrt{2} x-3$ (b) $y^{2}+\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{5} x-5$ 2. If on division of a polynomial $p(x)$ by a polynomial $g(x)$, the quotient is zero, what is the relation between the degrees of $p(x)$ and $g(x)$ ? 3. Can the quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+k x+k$ have equal zeroes for some odd integer $k>1$ ? 4. Given the $\sqrt{2}$ is a zero of the cubic polynomial $6 x^{3}+\sqrt{2} x^{2}-10 x-4 \sqrt{2}$, find its other two zeroes. 5. If one zero of the quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+3 x+k$ is 2 , then the value of $k$ is (a) 10 (b) -10 (c) 5 (d) -5 6. Given that two of the zeroes of the cubic polynomial $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ are 0 , the third zero is (a) $\frac{-b}{a}$ (b) $\frac{b}{a}$ (c) $\frac{c}{a}$ (d) $-\frac{d}{a}$ 7. If one of the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $(k-1) x^{2}+k x+1$ is -3 , then the value of $k$ is (a) $\frac{4}{3}$ (b) $\frac{-4}{3}$ (c) $\frac{2}{3}$ (d) $\frac{-2}{3}$ 8. The zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+99 x+127$ are (a) both positive (b) both negative (c) one positive and one negative (d) both equal ## Single Option Correct DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct. 1. The value of $x$, for which the polynomials $x^{2}-1$ and $x^{2}-2 x+1$ vanish simultaneously, is (a) 2 (b) -2 (c) -1 (d) 1 2. The value of the polynomial $x^{8}-x^{5}+x^{2}-x+1$ is (a) positive for all the real numbers (b) negative for all the real numbers (c) 0 (d) depends on value of $x$ 3. If $x=0 . \overline{7}$, then $2 x$ is (a) $1 . \overline{4}$ (b) $1 . \overline{5}$ (c) $1 . \overline{54}$ (d) $1 . \overline{45}$ 4. Lowest value of $x^{2}+4 x+2$ is (a) 0 (b) $\quad-2$ (c) 2 (d) 4 5. If $a^{3}-3 a^{2} b+3 a b^{2}-b^{3}$ is divided by $(a-b)$, then the remainder is (a) $a^{2}-a b+b^{2}$ (b) $a^{2}+a b+b^{2}$ (c) 1 (d) 0 6. A quadratic polynomial when divided by $x+2$ leaves a remainder of 1 and when divided by $x-1$, leaves a remainder of 4 . What will be the remainder if it is divided by $(x+2) (x-1)$ ? (a) 1 (b) 4 (c) $x+3$ (d) $x-3$ 7. If the sum of the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)=2 x^{3}-3 k x^{2}+4 x-5$ is 6 , then the value of $k$ is (a) 2 (b) -2 (c) 4 (d) -4 8. If the polynomial $x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-25 x+10$ is divided by another polynomial $x^{2}-2 x+k$, the remainder comes out to be $x+a$, find $k$ and $a$. [NTSE] (a) $3,-2$ (b) $5,-5$ (c) $-3,-5$ (d) $-5,1$ 9. If the polynomials $a x^{3}+4 x^{2}+3 x-4$ and $x^{3}-4 x+$ a leave the same remainder when divided by $x-3$, then the value of $a$ is [JSTSE] (a) 1 (b) -1 (c) $19 / 14$ (d) $-5 / 14$ 10. If the value of a quadratic polynomial $\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{x})$ is 0 only at $x=-1$ and $p(-2)=2$, then the value of $p(2)$ is [NTSE] (a) 18 (b) 9 (c) 6 (d) 3 11. Suppose the quadratic polynomial $p(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ has positive coefficients $a, b, c$ in arithmetic progression in that order. If $p(x)=0$ has integer roots $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Then, $\alpha+\beta+\alpha \beta$ is equal to [KVPY] (a) 3 (b) 5 (c) 7 (d) 14 12. If $x^{2}-4$ is the factor of $2 x^{3}+k_{1} x^{2}+k_{2} x+12$, where $k_{1}$, $k_{2}$ are constant, then the value of $k_{1}+k_{2}$ is [NTSE] (a) 11 (b) 5 (c) -11 (d) -5 13. If the polynomial $x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16^{2}-25 x+10$ is divided by another polynomial $x^{2}-2 x+k$, the remainder comes out to be $x+a$, then the value of $a$ is [NTSE] (a) -1 (b) -5 (c) 1 (d) 5 14. The number of real roots of the polynomial equation $x^{4}-x^{2}+2 x-1=0$ is [KVPY] (a) 0 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) 4 15. If $x=3+3^{2 / 3}+3^{1 / 3}$, then the value of $x^{3}-9 x^{2}+18 x-12$ is [JSTSE] (a) 1 (b) 0 (c) -1 (d) 2 16. Let $P(x)$ be a polynomial of degree 3 and $P(n)=\frac{1}{2}$ for $n=1,2,3,4$. Then the value of $P(5)$ is [NTSE] (a) 0 (b) $\frac{1}{5}$ (c) $-\frac{2}{5}$ (d) $\frac{3}{5}$ 17. Let $p(x)=x^{2}+a x+b$ have two distinct real roots, where $a, b$ are real numbers. Define $g(x)=p\left(x^{3}\right)$ for all real numbers $x$. Then which of the following statements are true? [KVPY] I. $g$ has exactly two distinct real roots II. $g$ can have more than two distinct real roots III. There exists a real number $\alpha$ such that $g(x) \geq \alpha$ for all real $x$ (a) Only I (b) Only I and III (c) Only II (d) Only II and III 18. The polynomial, $f(x)=(x-1)^{2}+(x-2)^{2}+(x-3)^{2}+(x -4)^{2}$ has minimum value, when $x=$ [NTSE] (a) 40 (b) 20 (c) 10 (d) 2.5 ## More Than One Option Correct DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONE OR MORE may be correct. 1. Which of the following given options is/are not correct? (a) $\frac{2}{x}+3$ is a polynomial (b) $\sqrt{x}+5$ is a polynomial (c) $\frac{2}{3 x-4}$ is a polynomial (d) $\sqrt{5} x^{2}+\frac{1}{2} x+\frac{3}{7}$ is a polynomial 2. Which of the following given options is/are not correct? (a) Degree of a zero polynomial is ' 0 ' (b) Degree of a zero polynomial is not defined (c) Degree of a constant polynomial is not defined (d) A polynomial of degree $n$ must have $n$ zeroes 3. Which of the following given options is/are incorrect? If $p(x)=q(x) g(x)+r(x)$ (By Division Algorithm) where $p(x), g(x)$ are any two polynomials with $g(x) \neq 0$, then (a) $r(x)=0$ always (b) degree of $r(x)<$ degree of $g(x)$ always (c) either $r(x)=0$ or degree of $r(x)<$ degree of $g(x)$ (d) $r(x)=g(x)$ 4. Which of the following is/are not a Polynomial? (a) $x^{2}+\frac{1}{x}$ (b) $2 x^{2}-3 \sqrt{x}+1$ (c) $x^{3}-3 x+1$ (d) $2 x^{\frac{3}{2}}-5 x$ 5. Which of the following is/are not graph of a quadratic polynomial? (a) ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-047.jpg?height=213&width=259&top_left_y=1190&top_left_x=383) (b) ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-047.jpg?height=223&width=259&top_left_y=1178&top_left_x=749) (c) ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-047.jpg?height=222&width=255&top_left_y=1430&top_left_x=383) (d) ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-047.jpg?height=227&width=259&top_left_y=1425&top_left_x=744) ## Passage Based Questions ## DIRECTIONS : Study the given paragraph (s) and answer the ## following questions. ## Passage - I If $\alpha, \beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $f(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$, then $\alpha+\beta=-\frac{b}{a}, \alpha \beta=\frac{c}{a}$ 1. If $\alpha, \beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $f(x)=x^{2}-p x+q$, then $\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}=$ (a) $p-q$ (b) $p+q$ (c) $\frac{p}{q}$ (d) $\frac{q}{p}$ 2. If $\alpha, \beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+x-2$, then $\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}-\frac{1}{\beta}\right)^{2}=$ (a) $\frac{9}{4}$ (b) $\frac{-9}{4}$ (c) $\frac{2}{5}$ (d) $\frac{-2}{5}$ 3. If $\alpha, \beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $f(x)=x^{2}-5 x+4$, then $\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}-2 \alpha \beta=$ (a) $\frac{27}{4}$ (b) $-\frac{27}{4}$ (c) $\frac{4}{27}$ (d) $-\frac{4}{27}$ Passage - II If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the zeroes of $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\frac{b}{a}, \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{c}{a}, \alpha \beta \gamma=-\frac{d}{a}$ 4. If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the zeroes of $x^{3}-5 x^{2}-2 x+24$ and $\alpha \beta=$ 12, then $\gamma=$ (a) 2 (b) -2 (c) 3 (d) -3 5. If $a-b, a, a+b$ are the roots of $x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+1$, then $a+b^{2}=$ (a) 3 (b) 4 (c) 5 (d) 2 6. If two zeroes of the polynomial $x^{3}-5 x^{2}-16 x+80$ are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, then zeroes are (a) $4,-4,5$ (b) $3,-3,5$ (c) $2,-2,5$ (d) $1,-1,5$ ## Assertion \& Reason DIRECTIONS : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements. (a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion. (b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion. (c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect. (d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct. 1. Assertion : If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the zeroes of $x^{3}-2 x^{2}+q x-r$ and $\alpha+\beta=0$, then $2 q=r$. Reason : If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the zeroes of $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\frac{b}{a}, \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{c}{a}, \alpha \beta \gamma=-\frac{d}{a}$. 2. Assertion : If one zero of polynomial $p(x)=\left(k^{2}+4\right) x^{2}+ 13 x+4 k$ is reciprocal of other, then $k=2$. Reason : If $(x-\alpha)$ is a factor of $p(x)$, then $p(\alpha)=0$ i.e. $\alpha$ is a zero of $p(x)$ 3. Assertion : $x^{3}+x$ has only one real zero. Reason : A polynomial of $n$th degree must have $n$ real zeroes. 4. Assertion : Degree of a zero polynomial is not defined. Reason : Degree of a non-zero constant polynomial is '0' 5. Assertion : Zeroes of $f(x)=x^{2}-4 x-5$ are $5,-1$. Reason : The polynomial whose zeroes are $2+\sqrt{3}, 2-\sqrt{3}$ is $x^{2}-4 x+7$. 6. Assertion : $x^{2}+4 x-5$ has two zeroes. Reason : A quadratic polynomial can have at the most two zeroes. ## Multiple Matching Questions DIRECTIONS : Following question has four statements ( $A, B, C$ and $D$ ) given in Column-I and statements ( $p, q, r, s \ldots .$. ) in Column-II. Any given statement in Column-I can have correct matching with one or more statement(s) given in Column-II. 1. | Column-I | Column-II
(Polynomials) | | :--- | :--- | | (Zeroes) | | | (A) $4-x^{2}$ | (p) 7 | | (B) $x^{3}-2 x^{2}$ | (q) -2 | | (C) $6 x^{2}-3-7 x$ | (r) 2 | | (D) $-x+7$ | (s) $3 / 2$ | | | | | | (t) 0 | | (u) $-1 / 3$ | | ## Integer Type Questions DIRECTIONS : Answer the following questions. The answer to each of the questions is a single digit integer, ranging from 0 to 9. 1. Obtain all zeroes of $x^{4}-3 x^{3}-7 x^{2}+9 x+12$ if two of its zeroes are $\pm \sqrt{3}$. Sum of zeroes is 2. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $p(s)=3 s^{2}-6 s+4$, the value of $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}+2\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)+3 \alpha \beta$ is 3. The value of $a b$ so that $x^{4}+x^{3}+8 x^{2}+a x+b$ is divisible by $x^{2}+1$, is 4. If $\alpha, \beta$ are the roots of the polynomial $f(x)=2 x^{2}+5 x+k$ satisfying the relation $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+\alpha \beta=\frac{21}{4}$, then find the value of $k$ for this to be possible. ## SOLUTIONS
(Brief Explanations of Selected Questions) ## Fill in the Blanks ## Multiple Choice Questions 1. (a) Since zeroes are reciprocal of each other, so product of the roots will be 1 , so $\frac{k+2}{k^{2}}=1$, $k^{2}-k-2=0 \Rightarrow(k-2)(k+1)=0$ $k=2, k=-1$, Since $k>0 \quad \therefore \quad k=2$ 2. (c) Let $\alpha, \beta$ be two zeroes of $2 x^{2}-8 x-\mathrm{m}$, where $a=\frac{5}{2}$. $\therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=\frac{(- \text { Coefficient of } \mathrm{x})}{\text { Coefficient of } \mathrm{x}^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{5}{2}+\mathrm{b}=\frac{8}{2}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=\frac{8}{2}-\frac{5}{2}=\frac{3}{2}$. 3. (d) Substitute $y=1$ 4. (a) Substitute $x=3$ in polynomial $a x^{3}+4 x^{2}+3 x-4$ and $x^{3}-4 x+a$ to obtain remainder and equate. $a(3)^{3}+4(3)^{2}+3(3)-4=(3)^{3}-4(3)+a \Rightarrow a=-1$ 5. (b) Equate value of polynomial at $x=a$ with a $a^{2}-27 a+196=a \Rightarrow a^{2}-28 a+196=0 \Rightarrow a=14$ 6. (b) Substitute $x=1$ in $f(x)$ and $x=-2$ in $g(x)$, and add $f(1)=2(1)-6(1)+4(1)-5=-5 \Rightarrow g(-2)=3(4)-9=3$ $f(1)+g(-2)=-2$ 7. (a) $x^{3}-3 x^{2}-10 x+24$ ∵ Last term $=$ (product of roots) ∴ Factorising $24=2 \times 4 \times 3$ Also sum of roots must be " 3 " ∴ Possible factors are ( $2,4,-3$ ) ∴ Factorization of $x^{3}-3 x^{2}-10 x+24$ $=(x-2)(x+3)(x-4)$ 8. (c) We have, $p(x)=x^{2}-10 x-75=x^{2}-15 x+5 x-75$ $=x(x-15)+5(x-15)=(x-15)(x+5)$ $\therefore p(x)=(x-15)(x+5)$ So, $p(x)=0$ when $x=15$ or $x=-5$. Therefore required zeroes are 15 and -5 . 9. (c) We have, sum of zeroes $=a+b=-\frac{(-4)}{2}=2$ Product of zeroes $=a b=\frac{3}{2}$ $\therefore a^{2} b+a b^{2}=a b(a+b)=\frac{3}{2} \times 2=3$ 10. (b) $x^{2}-(m+3) x+m x-m(m+3)=0$ $\Rightarrow x[x-(m+3)]+m[x-(m+3)]=0$ $\Rightarrow(x+m)[x-(m+3)]=0$ $$ \left.\begin{array}{l|r} \therefore & x+m=0 \\ & x=-m \end{array} \right\rvert\, \begin{aligned} x-(m+3)=0 \\ x=m+3 \end{aligned} $$ 1. linear, quadratic, cubic 2. $x$ 3. 3 4. $a, a$ 5. $a$ 6. zero 7. $n$ ## True / False 1. True [Dividend = (Divisor) × (Quotient) + Remainder $]$ 2. True 3. False, because the exponent of the variable is not a whole number. $\begin{aligned} & \text { 4. True, because } \\ & \text { 5. }\end{aligned} \frac{6 \sqrt{x}+x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\sqrt{x}}=6+x$, which is a polynomial. 4. False 5. False, a polynomial can have any number of zeroes. It depends upon the degree of the polynomial. 6. False, $x^{5}+1$ and $-x^{5}+2 x+3$ are two polynomials of degree 5 but the degree of the sum of the two polynomials is 1 . 7. True, $p(3)=0, p(-1)=0, p\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)=0$ 8. False 9. False, sum of zeroes $=\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}=\frac{-(-8)}{2}=4$ ## Match the Following 1. $(\mathrm{A}) \rightarrow \mathrm{q} ;(\mathrm{B}) \rightarrow \mathrm{s} ;(\mathrm{C}) \rightarrow \mathrm{r} ;(\mathrm{D}) \rightarrow \mathrm{p}$ 2. $\quad(\mathrm{A}) \rightarrow \mathrm{s}$; (B) $\rightarrow \mathrm{r}$; (C) $\rightarrow \mathrm{q}$; (D) $\rightarrow \mathrm{p}$ ## Very Short Answer Questions 1. $\frac{1}{\alpha^{3}}+\frac{1}{\beta^{3}}=\frac{\alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}}{(\alpha \beta)^{3}}=\frac{\frac{3 a b c-b^{3}}{a^{3}}}{\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)^{3}}$ $\left[\because \alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=(\alpha+\beta)^{3}-3 \alpha \beta(\alpha+\beta)\right]$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\alpha^{3}}+\frac{1}{\beta^{3}}=\frac{3 a b c-b^{3}}{c^{3}}$ 2. Let the quadratic polynomial be $a x^{2}+b x+c$, and its zeroes be $\alpha$ and $\beta$. We have $\alpha+\beta=-3=\frac{-b}{a}$ and $\alpha \beta=2=\frac{c}{a}$ If $a=1$, then $b=3$ and $c=2$. So, one quadratic polynomial which fits the given conditions is $x^{2}+3 x+2$. 3. $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}=\frac{6}{2}=3$ 4. (i) No zeroes (ii) No zeroes [ ∵ The curve does not cuts $x$-axis at any point] 5. (i) 2 zeroes [ ∵ curve crosses $x$-axis twice] (ii) 4 zeroes [curve cuts the $x$-axis at 4 points] 6. (i) $p(x)$ is of degree 2 as highest power term is $5 x^{2}$.i.e., $p(x)$ is quadratic. (ii) $q(x)$ is of degree 4 . (iii) $r(x)$ is of degree 6 . (iv) $h(x)$ is of degree 1. i.e. $h(x)$ is linear. 7. We have, $p(x)=x^{2}-10 x-75=x^{2}-15 x+5 x-75$ $$ =x(x-15)+5(x-15)=(x-15)(x+5) $$ $\therefore \quad p(x)=(x-15)(x+5)$ So, $p(x)=0$ when $x=15$ or $x=-5$. Therefore, required zeroes are 15 and -5 . 8. $2 x^{3}-4 x^{2}+5=\left(2 x^{2}-1\right)(x-2)+(x+3)$ Here, $$ \begin{array}{ll} p(x)=2 x^{3}-4 x^{2}+5 & \\ g(x)=2 x^{2}-1 & \\ q(x)=x-2 & {[\text { degree } q(x)=1]} \\ r(x)=x+3 & {[\text { degree } r(x)=1]} \end{array} $$ 9. Let $\alpha$ be one root, then $\frac{1}{\alpha}$ be other root, so product of root $=\frac{\text { Constant team }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}} \frac{1}{\alpha}=\frac{2 k}{k+1}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 1=\frac{2 k}{k+1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k+1=2 k \quad \Rightarrow \quad k-2 k=-1$ $\Rightarrow-k=-1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k=1$ 10. $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\left(\frac{-4}{1}\right) \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}-\sqrt{3}+\gamma=4$ $\Rightarrow \gamma=4$ Where, $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$ are three zeroes. ## Short Answer Questions 1. We have, $6 x^{2}-7 x-3=6 x^{2}-9 x+2 x-3$ $=3 x(2 x-3)+1(2 x-3)=(2 x-3)(3 x+1)$ Value of $6 x^{2}-3-7 x$ is zero when $2 x-3=0 \quad$ or $3 x+1=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{3}{2} \quad$ or $\quad x=-\frac{1}{3} \quad \therefore$ Zeroes are $\frac{3}{2}$ and $-\frac{1}{3}$ Now sum of zeroes $=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{9-2}{6}=\frac{7}{6}=-\frac{\text { coefficient of } x}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$ Product of zeroes $=\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{-1}{3}=\frac{-1}{2}=\frac{-3}{6}=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$ 2. $\because \alpha+\beta=\frac{-b}{a}$ and $\alpha \beta=\frac{c}{a}$ Also, $\alpha^{4}+\beta^{4}=\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}\right)^{2}-2 \alpha^{2} \beta^{2}$ $=\left[(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta\right]^{2}-2 \alpha^{2} \beta^{2}$ $\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}+\frac{\beta^{2}}{\alpha^{2}}=\frac{\alpha^{4}+\beta^{4}}{\alpha^{2} \beta^{2}}=\frac{\left(b^{2}-2 a c\right)^{2}-2 a^{2} c^{2}}{a^{4}\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)^{2}}$ $=\frac{b^{4}+4 a^{2} c^{2}-4 a b^{2} c-2 a^{2} c^{2}}{a^{4}\left(\frac{c^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)}$ $=\frac{b^{4}+2 a^{2} c^{2}-4 a b^{2} c}{a^{2} c^{2}}=2-\frac{4 b^{2}}{a c}+\frac{b^{4}}{a^{2} c^{2}}$ 3. Let $\alpha=5+\sqrt{3}, \beta=5-\sqrt{3}$ We know, $p(x)$ having zeroes as $\alpha, \beta$ is given by $p(x)=(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)$ $$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & p(x)=[x-(5+\sqrt{3})][x-(5-\sqrt{3})] \\ & =x^{2}-(5+\sqrt{3}) x-(5-\sqrt{3}) x+(5+\sqrt{3})(5-\sqrt{3}) \\ & =x^{2}-(5+\sqrt{3}+5-\sqrt{3}) x+\left(5^{2}-3\right) \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore \quad p(x)=x^{2}-10 x+22$ 4. Let $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$ be the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)$ such that $\alpha+\beta=0$. Now, $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}$ Since, $\gamma$ is a zero of the polynomial $f(x)$. Therefore, $f(\gamma)=0$ $\Rightarrow \gamma^{3}-\ell \gamma^{2}+m \gamma-n=0 \quad[\because \gamma=\ell]$ $\Rightarrow \ell^{3}-\ell^{3}+m \ell-n=0 \Rightarrow m \ell=n$ This is the required condition. 5. Since, the sum of the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $(a+1) x^{2}+(2 a+3) x+(3 a+4)$ is -1 . $\therefore \frac{-(2 a+3)}{a+1}=-1$ $\left[\because\right.$ Sum of zeroes $\left.=\frac{-(\text { Coefficient of } x)}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}\right]$ $\Rightarrow a+1=2 a+3 \Rightarrow a=-2$ Now, product of zeroes $\quad \frac{3 \quad 4}{-2+1}=\frac{3(-2)+4}{-2}$ $$ \left[\because \text { Product of zeroes }=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}\right] $$ 6. By actual division, we get, $\frac{a x^{3}+b x+c}{x^{2}+p x+1}=a x-a p+\frac{R(x)}{x^{2}+p x+1}$ Remainder polynomial, $R(x)=\left(b-a+a p^{2}\right) x+c+a p$ If $a x^{3}+b x+c$ has a factor of the form $x^{2}+p x+1$, then $R(x)$ must be identically zero, if $b-a+a p^{2}=0$ and $c+a p=0$ Eliminating $p$ from these equations, we get $b-a+a\left(-\frac{c}{a}\right)^{2}=0 \quad$ or $\quad a^{2}-c^{2}=a b$ 7. Polynomial is $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ and zeroes are $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$. Let $p=\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}+\frac{1}{\gamma} \Rightarrow p=\frac{\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha+\alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta \gamma} ; \alpha \beta \gamma=-d / a$ and $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=c / a \Rightarrow p=\frac{c / a}{-d / a}=-\frac{c}{d}$ 8. The given polynomial is $f(x)=x^{4}+2 x^{3}-2 x^{2}+x-1$. To make this polynomial exactly divisible by $x^{2}+2 x-3$, let us add $p$ then $x^{4}+2 x^{3}-2 x^{2}+x-1+p$ is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+2 x-3$. $$ \begin{gathered} x ^ { 2 } + 2 x - 3 \longdiv { x ^ { 4 } + 2 x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } + x - 1 + p ( x ^ { 2 } } \\ \frac{x^{4}+2 x^{3}-3 x^{2}}{x^{2}+x-1+p} \end{gathered} $$ For remainder to be zero, $x^{2}+2 x-3=x^{2}+x-1+p$ So, $p=x-2$, So, $x-2$ to be added to make the polynomial divisible by $x^{2}+2 x-3$. 9. Zeroes of polynomial $p(x)=x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+1$ are $a-b, a$ and $a+b$ ∵ sum of zeroes $=\frac{- \text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}$ So, $a-b+a+a+b=-\frac{(-3)}{1} \Rightarrow 3 a=3 \Rightarrow a=1$. $\alpha \beta \gamma=\frac{d}{a} \Rightarrow(a-b) a(a+b)=\frac{-1}{1}=-1$ $\Rightarrow(1-b) \times 1 \times(1+b)=-1 \quad[\because$ Put $a=1]$ $1-b^{2}=-1 \Rightarrow b^{2}=2 \Rightarrow b= \pm \sqrt{2} \quad$ So, $a=1, b= \pm \sqrt{2}$ 10. Given, $\alpha$ and $\frac{2}{\alpha}$ are the zeroes of $x^{2}+4 x+2 a$. Now Product of the zeroes $=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \alpha \times \frac{2}{\alpha}=\frac{2 a}{1} \\ & 2=2 a \\ \therefore \quad & a=1 \end{aligned} $$ 11. According to question, $$ \begin{gathered} x^{3}-5 x^{2}+6 x+4=g(x)(x-3)+4 \\ \Rightarrow g(x)=\frac{x^{3}-5 x^{2}+6 x}{x-3} \\ x - 3 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } - 5 x ^ { 2 } + 6 x } ( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \\ \frac{+x^{3}-3 x^{2}}{-2 x^{2}+6 x} \\ \frac{\frac{-2 x^{2}+6 x}{x}}{\frac{+}{x}} \end{gathered} $$ Hence, $g(x)=x^{2}-2 x$. 12. Let $f(x)=2 x^{4}-9 x^{3}+5 x^{2}+3 x-1$ It is given that $(2+\sqrt{3})$ and $(2-\sqrt{3})$ are zeroes of $f(x)$ ∴ By factor theorem, $$ \begin{aligned} & =(x-(2+\sqrt{3}))(x-(2-\sqrt{3})) \\ & =(x-2-\sqrt{3})(x-2+\sqrt{3}) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} & =(x-2)^{2}-\sqrt{3}^{2} \\ & =x^{2}+4-4 x-3 \\ & =x^{2}-4 x+1 \text { is factor of } f(x) \end{aligned} $$ Now, we divide $f(x)$ by $x^{2}-4 x+1$ to get then 2 fuctors. $$ \left.\begin{array}{l} x ^ { 2 } - 4 x + 1 \longdiv { 2 x ^ { 4 } - 9 x ^ { 3 } + 5 x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 1 } ( 2 x ^ { 2 } - x - 1 \\ \frac{2 x^{4}-8 x^{3}+2 x^{2}}{+\quad-} \\ \begin{array}{c} -x^{3}+3 x^{2}+3 x-1 \\ \frac{-x^{3}+4 x^{2}-x}{+\quad+} \end{array} \\ \frac{-x^{2}+4 x-1}{+x^{2}+4 x-1}+\frac{0}{+} \end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} & \therefore f(x)=\left(x^{2}-4 x+1\right)\left(2 x^{2}-x-1\right) \\ & {[x-(2+\sqrt{3})][x-(2-\sqrt{3})]\left(2 x^{2}+x-2 x-1\right)} \\ & =[x-(2+\sqrt{3})][x-(2-\sqrt{3})][x(2 x+1)-1(2 x+1)] \\ & =(x-(2+\sqrt{3}))(x-(2-\sqrt{3}))(x-1)(2 x+1) \end{aligned} $$ Hence, zeroes of $f(x)$ are $2+\sqrt{3}, 2-\sqrt{3}, 1, \frac{-1}{2}$ 13. Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the polynomial $x^{2}-(k+6) x+2(2 k-1)$. given, $\alpha+\beta=\frac{\alpha \beta}{2}$ $\mathrm{k}+6=\frac{2(2 \mathrm{k}-1)}{2}[\because \alpha+\beta=k+6$ and $\alpha \beta=2(2 k-1]$ $\Rightarrow k+6=2 k-1 \Rightarrow 6+1=2 k-k \Rightarrow k=7$. Therefore, the value of $k$ is 7 . 14. | Polynomials | Not polynomials | Quadratic polynomials | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $2 x+3$
$3 x^{2}+7 x+2$
$4 x^{3}+3 x^{2}+2$
$7 x+\sqrt{7}$
$5 x^{3}-7 x+2$
$5 x-\frac{1}{2}$
$a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ | $x^{3}+\sqrt{3 x}+7$ $2 x^{2}+3-\frac{5}{x}$
$x+\frac{1}{x}$ | $3 x^{2}+7 x+2$ | (i) Number of not polynomials $=3$ (ii) Number of quadratic polynomials $=1$ ## Long Answer Questions 1. Let $p(x)=x^{3}-2 x^{2}-x+2$ Comparing it with $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$, We get, $\quad a=1, b=-2, c=-1, d=2$ Now, $p(2)=2^{3}-2(2)^{2}-2+2=8-8-2+2=0$ $p(1)=1^{3}-2(1)^{2}-1+2=1-2-1+2=0$ $$ p(-1)=(-1)^{3}-2(-1)^{2}+1+2=-1-2+1+2=0 $$ $\Rightarrow 2,1,-1$ are the zeroes of the polynomial $p(n)$ Let $\alpha=2, \beta=1, \gamma=-1$ $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=2+1-1=2=\frac{(-2)}{1}=-\frac{b}{a}$ $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=2 \times 1+1(-1)+(-1) 2$ $=2-1-2=-1=-\frac{1}{1}=\frac{c}{a}$ $\alpha \beta \gamma=2(1)(-1)=-2=-\frac{2}{1}=-\frac{d}{a}$ 2. $f(x)=a b x^{2}+\left(b^{2}-a c\right) x-b c=a b x^{2}+b^{2} x-a c x-b c$ $=b x(a x+b)-c(a x+b)=(a x+b)(b x-c)$ Thus, the zeroes of $f(x)$ are $\alpha=-\frac{b}{a}$ and $\beta=\frac{c}{b}$. Now, $\alpha+\beta=-\frac{b}{a}+\frac{c}{b}=\frac{a c-b^{2}}{a b}$ and $\alpha \beta=-\frac{b}{a} \times \frac{c}{b}=-\frac{c}{a}$ From the given polynomial $f(x)$, sum of zeroes : $\alpha+\beta=-\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}=-\left(\frac{b^{2}-a c}{a b}\right)=\frac{a c-b^{2}}{a b}$ And product of zeroes : $\alpha \beta=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}=-\frac{b c}{a b}=-\frac{c}{a}$ Hence, the sum and product of the zeroes are same in both cases. Therefore the relationship between the zeroes and its coefficients is verified. 3. By division theorem, Dividend $=$ Divisor × Quotient + Remainder $\therefore x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+2=h(x) \times(x-2)+(-2 x+4)$ $\Rightarrow h(x) \times(x-2)=x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+2-(-2 x+4)$ $\Rightarrow h(x)=\frac{x^{3}-3 x^{2}+3 x-2}{x-2}$ To find $h(x)$, divide $x^{3}-3 x^{2}+3 x-2$ by $x-2$ as follows $$ \begin{gathered} x - 2 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 2 } x ^ { 2 } - x + 1 \\ \frac{x^{3}-2 x^{2}}{-+} \\ \frac{\begin{array}{r} -x^{2}+3 x \\ -x^{2}+2 x \\ +- \\ x-2 \\ -+ \\ 0 \end{array}}{\frac{1}{-2}} \end{gathered} $$ Hence, $h(x)=x^{2}-x+1$. 4. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the cubic polynomial $x^{2}+4 x+3$ then, $\alpha+\beta=-4$ and $\alpha \beta=3$ Sum of zeroes $=1+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}+1+\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$ $=\frac{\alpha \beta+\beta^{2}+\alpha \beta+\alpha^{2}}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+2 \alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta}$ $=\frac{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{(-4)^{2}}{3}=\frac{16}{3}$ Product of zeroes $=\left(1+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\right)\left(1+\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)$ $=1+\frac{\alpha}{\beta}+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}+\frac{\alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta}$ $\frac{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+2 \alpha \beta}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{(-4)^{2}}{3}=\frac{16}{3}$ So, required polynomial $=x^{2}-$ (Sum of the zeroes) $x+$ Product of the zeroes $=x^{2}-\left(\frac{16}{3}\right) x+\frac{16}{3}=\left(3 x^{2}-16 x+16\right) \frac{1}{3}$ 5. Factors of $x^{2}+7 x+12$ : $$ \begin{aligned} & x^{2}+7 x+12=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x^{2}+4 x+3 x+12=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (x+4)(x+3)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x=-4,-3 \end{aligned} $$ Let $p(x)=x^{4}+7 x^{3}+7 x^{2}+p x+q$ If $p(x)$ is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+7 x+12$, then $x=-4$ and $x=-3$ are zeroes of $p(x)$ [from eq (i)] $p(x)=x^{4}+7 x^{3}+7 x^{2}+p x+q$ $p(-4)=(-4)^{4}+7(-4)^{3}+7(-4)^{2}+p(-4)+q$ but $p(-4)=0$ $\therefore 0=256-448+112-4 p+q$ $\Rightarrow \quad 0=-4 p+q-80$ $\Rightarrow 4 p-q=-80$ and $p(-3)=(-3)^{4}+7(-3)^{3}+7(-3)^{2}+p(-3)+q$ but $p(-3)=0$ $\therefore 0=81-189+63-3 p+q$ $\Rightarrow 0=-3 p+q-45$ $\Rightarrow \quad 3 p-q=-45$ By elimination method $4 p-q=-80$ $3 p-q=-45$ $-\quad+\quad+$ $P=-35$ Put the value of $p$ in eq. (i), $$ \begin{array}{ll} & 4(-35)-q=-80 \\ \Rightarrow & -140-q=-80 \\ & -q=140-80 \\ \therefore & -q=60 \\ \text { Hence, } & q=-60 \\ \text { H } & p=-35, q=-60 \end{array} $$ 6. $\quad x=-1$ and $x=-3$ are zeroes. $\therefore(x+1)(x+3)=x^{2}+4 x+3$ $$ \begin{gathered} x ^ { 2 } + 4 x + 3 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } + 5 x ^ { 2 } + 7 x + 3 ( x + 1 } \\ \frac{x^{3}+4 x^{2}+3 x}{-} \\ \frac{x^{2}+4 x+3}{x^{2}+4 x+3}-- \\ 0 \end{gathered} $$ $$ x+1=0 $$ Remainder is 0 $$ \therefore \quad(n+1) \text { is a factor } $$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=-1$ $\therefore \quad$ The third zero is -1 . ## Reasoning Based Questions 1. Since, HCF of $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ is $3 x-2$ and it was found in two steps by long division method. Then, according to the question $p(x)=q(x)(x+2)+(3 x-2)[\because$ degree $p(x)>$ degree $q(x)]$ $q(x)=(3 x-2)(2 x+1)=6 x^{2}-x-2$ Now, $p(x)=q(x)(x+2)+(3 x-2)$ $=\left(6 x^{2}-x-2\right)(x+2)+(3 x-2)$ $=6 x^{3}+12 x^{2}-x^{2}-2 x-2 x-4+3 x-2$ $=6 x^{3}+11 x^{2}-x-6$ 2. $f(x)=x^{2}+6 x+a, g(x)=x^{2}+4 x+b$, $h(x)=x^{2}+14+c$ $\mathrm{LCM}=(x+8)(x-2)(x+6)$ Since, three given polynomials are quadratic and their LCM is cubic, then each polynomial is the product of two factors of LCM. By inspection method, $f(x)=(x+8)(x-2) \Rightarrow x^{2}+6 x+a$ $=(x+8)(x-2) \Rightarrow a=-16$ $g(x)=(x-2)(x+6) \Rightarrow x^{2}+4 x+b$ $=(x-2)(x+6) \Rightarrow b=-12$ $h(x)=(x+8)(x+6) \Rightarrow x^{2}+14 x+c$ $=(x+8)(x+6) \Rightarrow c=48$ Hence, $a+b+c=-16-12+48=20$. 3. Divide the polynomial $x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}-1$ by $x^{2}+x-1$ using long division method, $$ \begin{aligned} & x^{2}+x-1 \sqrt{x^{2}+(p-1) x+(q+2-p)} \\ & \frac{x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}-1}{(p-1) x^{3}+(q+1) x^{2}-1} \\ & \frac{-(p-1) x^{3}+(p-1) x^{2}-(p-1) x}{-\quad+} \\ & \frac{-(q+2-p) x^{2}+(p-1) x-1}{(q+2-p) x^{2}+(q+2-p) x-(q+2-p)}+ \\ &(2 p-q-3) x+(q-p+1) \end{aligned} $$ Since, $x^{2}+x-1$ is a factor of the polynomial $x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}-1$, then remainder must be zero. Then, $$ (2 p-q-3) x+(q-p+1)=0 $$ As the remainder is zero, then coefficient of $x$ and constant term must be zero. So, $2 p-q-3=0$ and $q-p+1=0$ $$ \begin{aligned} & 2 p-q=3 \\ & p-q=1 \end{aligned} $$ From (i) - (ii); $$ p=2 $$ Now, substitute the value of $p$ in eqn. (ii), then $$ 2-q=1 \Rightarrow q=2-1=1 $$ Hence, required value of $p$ is 2 and $q$ is 1 . 4. It is given that $f(x)=(x+2)^{7}(x+4)^{\mathrm{p}}$ $g(x)=(x+2)^{4}(x+4)^{9}$ LCM of $f(x)$ and $g(x)=(x+2)^{q}(x+4)^{9}$ $\therefore q \geq 7$ and $p \leq 9$ For maximum value of $p-q, p$ is maximum and $q$ is minimum. Hence, maximum value of $p-q$ is $9-7=2$. 5. Let $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ be the zeroes of the given polynomial $x^{3}-5 x^{2}-2 x+24$ such that $\alpha \beta=12$ ∵ Sum of the zeroes $=-\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}$ $\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\frac{(-5)}{1}=5$ $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{3}}$ $\Rightarrow \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{-2}{1}=-2$ and product of the zeroes $=-\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient } x^{3}}$ $\Rightarrow \alpha \beta \gamma=-\frac{24}{1}=-24 \quad(\because \alpha \beta=12)$ $\Rightarrow(12) \gamma=-24$ $\Rightarrow \quad \gamma=-\frac{24}{12}=-2$ Substituting this value of $\gamma$ in (ii), we get $\alpha+\beta-2=5 \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=7$ Also, $(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta=7^{2}-4 \times 12=49-48$ $\Rightarrow(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=1 \Rightarrow \alpha-\beta=1$ or -1 . Case I. Taking $\alpha-\beta=1$ Add (iv) and (v), to get $2 \alpha=8 \Rightarrow \alpha=4$ and then from (iv), $\beta=7-\alpha=7-4=3$. Hence, we get $\quad \alpha=4, \beta=3, \gamma=-2$. Case II. Taking $\alpha-\beta=-1$ Add (iv) and (vi) to get $2 \alpha=6 \Rightarrow \alpha=3$ and then from (iv), $$ \beta=7-\alpha=7-3=4 $$ Hence, $\alpha=3, \beta=4, \gamma=-2$. ## HOTS Questions 1. Since, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are zeroes of polynomial $f(x)=2 x^{2}-5 x+7$. So, $\alpha+\beta=-\left(-\frac{5}{2}\right)=\frac{5}{2}$ and $\alpha \beta=\frac{7}{2}$ Let $S$ and $P$ denote respectively the sum and product of zeroes of the required polynomial. Then, polynomial is $p(x)=k\left(x^{2}-S x+P\right)$ $\mathrm{S}=(2 \alpha+3 \beta)+(3 \alpha+2 \beta)=5(\alpha+\beta)=5 \times \frac{5}{2}=\frac{25}{2}$ and, $P=(2 \alpha+3 \beta)(3 \alpha+2 \beta)$ $\Rightarrow P=6\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}\right)+13 \alpha \beta=6 \alpha^{2}+6 \beta^{2}+12 \alpha \beta+\alpha \beta$ $=6(\alpha+\beta)^{2}+\alpha \beta$ $\Rightarrow P=6 \times\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{7}{2}=\frac{75}{2}+\frac{7}{2}=41$ Hence, the required polynomial is given by $p(x)=k\left(x^{2}-S x+P\right)$ or, $p(x)=k\left(x^{2}-\frac{25}{2} x+41\right)$, where $k$ is any non-zero real number. 2. Let the cubic polynomial be $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ Let $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ be the zeroes of the required cubic polynomial, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=\frac{-b}{a} ; \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{c}{a} ;$ and $\alpha \beta \gamma=\frac{-d}{a}$. Now, according to the question, $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=2$ $\Rightarrow \frac{-b}{a}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=-2 \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=-7 \Rightarrow \frac{c}{a}=-7$ And $\quad \alpha \beta \gamma=-14 \Rightarrow \frac{-d}{a}=-14 ; \frac{d}{a}=14$ If $a=1$, then $b=-2, c=-7, d=14$ Substituting the values of $a, b, c$ and $d$ in (i), we get the required cubic polynomial $x^{3}-2 x^{2}-7 x+14$ This polynomial satisfies all the given conditions. 3. Let $y=f(x)$ or , $y=3-2 x-x^{2}$ Let us list a few values of $y=3-2 x-x^{2}$ corresponding to a few values of $x$ as follows: | $\mathbf{x}$ | -5 | -4 | -2 | -1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | $\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{3}-\mathbf{2} \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{2}}$ | -12 | -5 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 0 | -5 | -12 | -21 | Thus, the following points lie on the graph of polynomial $y=3-2 x-x^{2}$ : $(-5,-12),(-4,-5),(-3,0),(-2,3),(-1,4),(0,3),(1,0)$, $(2,-5),(3,-12)$ and $(4,-21)$ Let us plot these points on a graph paper. The curve thus obtained represents a parabola, as shown in figure. ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-054.jpg?height=538&width=782&top_left_y=374&top_left_x=1023) 4. $\quad a=-1, b=-2$ 1 and 2 are the zeroes of $q(x)$ which are not the zeroes of $p(x)$.

case_studies.description:
1. (i) (d) parabola
(ii) (a) 2
(iii) (b) $-1,3$
(iv) (c) $\quad \mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}-3$
(v) (d) 0
2. (i) (a) Sum of zeroes $=-\frac{\operatorname{coefficient} \text { of } x}{\operatorname{coefficient} \text { of } x^{2}}$

$$
=-\frac{(-12)}{4}=3
$$

(ii) (d) Product of zeroes $=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$

$$
=\frac{c}{a}
$$

(iii) (c) ∵ One zero of the polynomial is four times the other

$$
\therefore \beta=4 \alpha
$$

(iv) (d) $\alpha+\beta=-\frac{b}{a}=3$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \alpha+\beta=3 \Rightarrow \alpha+4 \alpha=3 \quad[\because \beta=4 \alpha] \\
\Rightarrow & \alpha=\frac{3}{5}
\end{array}
$$

(v) (b) $\because \alpha \beta=\frac{3 k+8}{4}$

$$
\begin{array}{ll} 
& \alpha \cdot(4 \alpha)=\frac{3 k+8}{4} \quad[\because \beta=4 \alpha] \\
& 4 \alpha^{2}=\frac{3 k+8}{4} \\
& 4\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}=\frac{36}{25}=\frac{3 k+8}{4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & 144=75 k+200 \\
\Rightarrow \quad & k=\frac{144-200}{75}=-\frac{56}{75} \\
\therefore \quad & k=-\frac{56}{75}
\end{array}
$$

## Text-book Questions

1. (i) Graph of $y=p(x)$ does not intersect the $x$-axis.

2. (i) We have, $6 x^{2}-3-7 x=6 x^{2}-7 x-3$
$=6 x^{2}-9 x+2 x-3=3 x(2 x-3)+1(2 x-3)$
$=(3 x+1)(2 x-3)$
∴ The zeroes of $6 x^{2}-3-7 x$ are $-\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{3}{2}$.
Therefore, sum of the zeroes
$=-\frac{1}{3}+\frac{3}{2}=\frac{7}{6}=\frac{- \text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}$
and, product of zeroes $=\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)$

$$
=\frac{-1}{2}=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}
$$

(ii) We have, $p(u)=4 u^{2}+8 u=0$.
$\Rightarrow p(u)=4 u(u+2)$
So, the value of $p(u)=4 u^{2}+8 u$ is zero,
when $u=0$ or $u=-2$
The zeroes of $4 u^{2}+8 \mathrm{u}$ are $\mathrm{u}=0$ or $u=-2$
Sum of its zeroes $=0+(-2)=-2$
$=\frac{-8}{4}=\frac{-(\text { Coefficient of } u)}{\text { Coefficient of } u^{2}}$
Product of its zeroes $=0(-2)=0$
$=\frac{0}{4}=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } u^{2}}$
3. (i) $-\frac{b}{a}=\sqrt{2}, \frac{c}{a}=\frac{1}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=\frac{-\sqrt{2}}{1}, \frac{c}{a}=\frac{1}{3}$
Select $a=$ L.C.M. $(1,3)=3$.
Then $\frac{b}{3}=-\sqrt{2}$ and $\frac{c}{3}=\frac{1}{3}$
$\Rightarrow b=-3 \sqrt{2}$ and $c=1$.
Substituting $a=3, b=-3 \sqrt{2}$ and $c=1$
in $a x^{2}+b x+c$,
we get the required polynomial $3 x^{2}-3 \sqrt{2} x+1$.
(ii) Let the polynomial be $a x^{2}+b x+c$ and its zero be $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Then,
$\alpha+\beta=-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{-b}{a}$ and, $\alpha \beta=\frac{1}{4}=\frac{c}{a}$
If $a=4$, then $b=-1$ and $c=1$.
∴ One quadratic polynomial which fits the given conditions is $4 x^{2}+x+1$.

1. (a) $4 x^{2}+5 \sqrt{2} x-3=4 x^{2}+6 \sqrt{2} x-\sqrt{2} x-3$
$=2 \sqrt{2} \cdot x(\sqrt{2} \cdot x+3)-1(\sqrt{2} x+3)=(2 \sqrt{2} x-1)(\sqrt{2} x+3)$
The zeroes of the given polynomial are $x=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$ and
$-\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$. Sum of the roots $=-\frac{b}{a}=\frac{-5 \sqrt{2}}{4}$
Sum $=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}-\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}-6 \sqrt{2}}{2 \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}} \quad(\because a=4, b=5 \sqrt{2})$
$=\frac{-5 \sqrt{2}}{4}=\frac{-b}{a}$. Product of the roots $=\frac{c}{a}=\frac{-3}{4}$
Product $=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \times-\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}(\because a=4, c=-3)$
$=\frac{-3}{4}=\frac{c}{a}$
Hence verified the relation between the zeroes and the coefficients of the polynomial.
(b) $y^{2}+\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{5} y-5=2 y^{2}+3 \sqrt{5} y-10$
$=2 y^{2}+4 \sqrt{5} y-\sqrt{5} y-10=2 y(y+2 \sqrt{5})-\sqrt{5}(y+2 \sqrt{5})$
$=(2 y-\sqrt{5})(y+2 \sqrt{5})$

The zeroes of the given polynomial are $y=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$ and $-2 \sqrt{5}$.
Sum of the roots $=-\frac{b}{a}=-\frac{3 \sqrt{5}}{2}$
Sum $=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}-2 \sqrt{5}=\frac{-3 \sqrt{5}}{2}=-\frac{b}{a}$
Product of the roots $=\frac{c}{a}=-5$
Product $=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} \times-2 \sqrt{5}=-\sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{5}=-5$

$$

-+-

& +4 x^{3}+8 x^{2}-4 k x \\
+\quad+ & \begin{array}{c}

(8-k) x^{2}-(16-2 k) x+\left(8 k^{2}-k\right) \\
+ \\
-
\end{array} \\
& (-10+2 k) x+\left(10-a-8 k+k^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $f(x)-(x+a)=x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-26 x+10-a$ to be exactly divisible by $x^{2}-2 x+k$, we must have $(-10+2 k) x+\left(10-a-8 k+k^{2}\right)=0$ for all $x$. Equating co-efficients of $x$ and constant terms

(D) $x=7$

## Integer Type Questions

1. Ans : 3
$\pm \sqrt{3}$ are zeroes $\Rightarrow(x+\sqrt{3})(x-\sqrt{3})=x^{2}-3$ is a divisor of $x^{4}-3 x^{3}-7 x^{2}+9 x+12$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm { x } ^ { 2 } - 3 \longdiv { x ^ { 4 } - 3 x ^ { 3 } - 7 x ^ { 2 } + 9 x + 1 2 } ( x ^ { 2 } - 3 x - 4 \\
& \frac{x^{4}+3 x^{2}}{-3 \mathrm{x}^{3}-4 \mathrm{x}^{2}+9 \mathrm{x}+12} \\
& \frac{-3 \mathrm{x}^{2}+9 \mathrm{x}}{+\mathrm{x}^{2}+12} \\
& q(x)=x^{2}-3 x-\frac{-4 \mathrm{x}^{2}+12}{+-4=x^{2}-4 x+x-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \quad=x(x-4)+1(x-4) \\
& q(x)=(x+1)(x-4) \\
& \text { zeroes of } q(x) \text { are }-1,4
\end{aligned}
$$

∴ Remaining required zeroes are $-1,4$.
Sum $=-1+4=3$
2. Ans : 8

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are zeroes of quadratic polynomial
$p(s)=3 s^{2}-6 s+4$
So, $\alpha+\beta=-\left(\frac{-6}{3}\right)=2$ and $\alpha \beta=4 / 3$
Let $x=\frac{\alpha}{\beta}+\frac{\beta}{\alpha}+2\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)+3 \alpha \beta$
$=\frac{\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}}{\alpha \beta}+2\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha \beta}\right)+3 \alpha \beta$
We know, $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta=2^{2}-2 \times \frac{4}{3}$
$=4-\frac{8}{3}=\frac{4}{3}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=\frac{4}{3}, \alpha+\beta & =2 \text { and } \alpha \beta=4 / 3 . \text { So, } x=\frac{4 / 3}{4 / 3} \\
& +2\left(\frac{2}{4 / 3}\right)+3 \times \frac{4}{3}=1+4 \times \frac{3}{4}+4=8
\end{aligned}
$$

3. Ans: 7

If $x^{4}+x^{3}+8 x^{2}+a x+b$ is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+1$, then the remainder should be zero.
On dividing the polynomial by $x^{2}+1$, we get,

$$
\begin{gathered}
x ^ { 2 } + 1 \longdiv { x ^ { 4 } + x ^ { 3 } + 8 x ^ { 2 } + a x + b } x ^ { 2 } + x + 7 \\
\frac{-x^{4}+x^{2}}{x^{3}+7 x^{2}+a x+b} \\
\frac{-x^{3}+x}{7 x^{2}+x(a-1)+b} \\
\frac{-7 x^{2}+7}{x(a-1)+b-7}
\end{gathered}
$$

$\therefore \quad$ Quotient $=x^{2}+x+7$ and
Remainder $=x(a-1)+(2-7)$
Now, since, $x^{2}+1$ is factor of this polynomial,
$\therefore \quad$ Remainder $=0$
$\Rightarrow x(a-1)+(b-7)=0 \Rightarrow x(a-1)+(b-7)=0 x+0$
Equating coefficient of $x$ and constant term, we get,
$a-1=0$ and $b-7=0 \Rightarrow a=1$ and $\mathrm{b}=7$
$\therefore a b=1 \times 7=7$
4. Ans : 2
$\alpha+\beta=-\frac{5}{2}$ and $\alpha \beta=\frac{k}{2}$
$\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+\alpha \beta=\frac{21}{4}\left[\because \alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta\right]$
$\Rightarrow(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-\alpha \beta=\frac{21}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{25}{4}-\frac{k}{2}=\frac{21}{4} \Rightarrow k=2$

## (CONCEPT M A <br> A P )

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-060.jpg?height=762&width=891&top_left_y=1788&top_left_x=322)

| Algebraic Method |
| :--- |
| Consider the following system of equation <br> There are following three methods under Algebraic method to solve the above system: <br> (i) Substitution method <br> (a) Find the value of one variable, say y in terms of x or x in terms of y <br> (b) Substitute this value in second equation to get equation in one variable <br> (c) Now substitute the value/solution so obtained in the equation got <br> (ii) Elimination Method <br> Write equation as $a_{1} x+b_{1} y+c_{1}=0 ; a_{2} x+b_{2} y+c_{2}=0$ from one equation. and find solution. in step(a) <br> (a) If coefficient of one variable are not same in both the equation multiply both the equation with suitable non-zero constants to make coefficient of one variable numerically equal. <br> (b) Add or subtract the equation to get equation in one variable and solve it. <br> (c) Now substitute the value got in the above step in either of the original equation to get value of the other variable. <br> (iii) Cross multiplication method <br> Consider the following diagram.![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-060.jpg?height=468&width=125&top_left_y=904&top_left_x=974) $\frac{x}{b_{1} c_{2}-b_{2} c_{1}}=\frac{y}{c_{1} a_{2}-c_{2} a_{1}}=\frac{1}{a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1}}$ <br> Solve it to get the solution provided, $a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1} \neq 0$ |


| Graphical Method |
| :--- |
| Consider the standard form of linear equations in two variables. $a_{1} x+b_{1} y+c_{1}=0 ; a_{2} x+b_{2} y+c_{2}=0$ <br> While solving the above system of equation by this method following three cases arise. <br> (i) If $\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}} \neq \frac{b_{1}}{b_{2}}$; system is called consistent, having one or unique solution and pair of straight lines representing the above equations intersect at one point only. <br> (ii) If $\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{b_{1}}{b_{2}}=\frac{c_{1}}{c_{2}}$; system is called dependent and have infinitely many solution. Pair of lines representing the equations coincide. <br> (iii) If $\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{b_{1}}{b_{2}} \neq \frac{c_{1}}{c_{2}}$; system is called inconsistent and has no solution. Pair of lines representing the equations are parallel or do not intersect at any point. |

## Equations Reducible to a Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

By using the suitable substitution or simplification first we convert the given system into the system of a pair of linear equations in two variables. Then after using any algebraic or graphical method we solve the system.

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1
examples.problem:
Which of the following expressions are polynomials ?
(a) $5 x^{2}-3 x+9$
(b) $\frac{4}{3} x^{7}-5 x^{4}+3 x^{2}-1$
examples.solution: Both are polynomials.

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 2
examples.problem:
Write down the degree of the following polynomials.
(a) $3 x+5$
(b) $3 t^{2}-5 t+9 t^{4}$
(c) $\quad 2-y^{2}-y^{3}+2 y^{8}$

SOLUTION :
(a) The highest power term is $3 x$ and exponent of $x$ is $1 . \therefore$ degree of polynomial $3 x+5$ is 1
(b) The highest power term is $9 t^{4}$ and exponent of $t$ is 4 . So, degree of the given polynomial is 4 .
(c) The highest power term is $2 y^{8}$ and exponent of $y$ is 8 .

## VALUE OF A POLYNOMIAL

A polynomial of $x$ can be represented by $f(x), g(x), p(x), q(x)$, etc. The value of a polynomial $f(x)$ at $x=\alpha$ is obtained by substituting $x=\alpha$ in the given polynomial and is denoted by $f(\alpha)$.
Consider the polynomial : $p(x)=5 x^{3}-2 x^{2}+3 x-2$
If we replace $x$ by 1 everywhere in $p(x)$, we get
$p(1)=5 \times(1)^{3}-2 \times(1)^{2}+3 \times(1)-2=5-2+3-2=4$
So, we say that the value of $p(x)$ at $x=1$ is 4 .

## ZERO(ES) / ROOT(S) OF POLYNOMIALS

Let $x=r$ be a root or zero of a polynomial $P(x)$, if $P(r)=0$.
In other words, $x=r$ is a root or zero of a polynomial if it is a solution to the equation $P(x)=0$
The process of finding the zeroes of $P(x)$ is actually solving the equation $P(x)=0$
Let's first find the zeroes of $P(x)=x^{2}+2 x-15$. To find the zeroes, we simply solve the following equation.
$x^{2}+2 x-15=0 \Rightarrow(x+5)(x-3)=0 \Rightarrow x=-5,3$
So, this second degree polynomial has two zeroes or roots -5 and 3 .

examples.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3
examples.problem: If $x=2$ and $x=0$ are roots of the polynomial $f(x)=2 x^{3}-5 x^{2}+a x+b$. Find the values of $a$ and $b$.
examples.solution:
Since, 2 is a root, therefore $$ f(2)=2(2)^{3}-5(2)^{2}+a(2)+b=0 \quad \Rightarrow 16-20+2 a+b=0 \Rightarrow 2 a+b=4 $$ Since 0 is also a root, therefore $$ f(0)=2(0)^{3}-5(0)^{2}+a(0)+b=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b=0 \Rightarrow 2 a=4 \Rightarrow a=2, b=0 $$ Time to Check Your Knowledge If the zeroes of $p(x)=x^{3}+2 x^{2}+a$, are also the zeroes of polynomial $q(x)=x^{5}-x^{4}-4 x^{3}+3 x^{2}+3 x+b$; then find the values of $a$ and $b$. Which zeroes of $q(x)$ are not the zeroes of $p(x)$ ? If zeroes of $p(x)$ are also the zeroes of $q(x)$, then $q(x)$ is divisible by $p(x)$. Let us now divide $q(x)$ by $p(x)$ to obtain the remainder. \[ \] If $q(x)$ is divisible by $p(x)$, then remainder must be 0 . Now, remainder $=0$ $\Rightarrow(-1-a) x^{2}+3 x(1+a)+b-2 a=0$ for all $x$. $\Rightarrow(-1-a)=0,3(1+a)=0$ and $b-2 a=0 \Rightarrow a=-1$ and $\mathrm{b}=-2$ Substituting $\mathrm{a}=-1$ in $p(x)$, we obtain $p(x)=x^{3}+2 x^{2}+1$ Now, $x^{2}-3 x+2=(x-1)(x-2)$. So, zeroes of $x^{2}-3 x+2$ are 1 and 2 . $q(1)=2 \neq 0$ and $q(2)=15 \neq 0$ So, zeroes of the $x^{2}-3 x+2$ are not the zeroes of $p(x)$. Hence, 1 and 2 are zeroes of $q(x)$ which are not the zeroes of $p(x)$. ## GEOMETRICAL MEANING OF THE ZERO(ES) OF A POLYNOMIAL Zero(es) of a polynomial is/are the $x$-coordinate of the point(s) where the graph of $y=f(x)$ intersects the $x$-axis. ## GRAPHS OF POLYNOMIALS Graph of a polynomial $f(x)$ is a smooth free hand curve passing through points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right),\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right), \ldots \ldots \ldots$. etc., where $y_{1}$, $y_{2}, y_{3}, \ldots \ldots .$. are the values of the polynomial $f(x)$ at $x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}, \ldots \ldots$. respectively. In order to draw the graph of a polynomial $f(x)$, we may follow the following algorithm. ## Algorithm to draw the graph of the polynomials : Step 1: Find the values $y_{1}, y_{2}, \ldots \ldots . y_{n}$, that is value of polynomial $f(x)$ at $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots \ldots . . x_{n}$ and prepare a table that gives values of $y$ or $f(x)$ for various values of $x$. | $x$ | $x_{1}$ | $x_{2}$ | $\cdots \cdots$ | $x_{n}$ | $x_{n+1}$ | $\cdots \cdots$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $y=f(x)$ | $y_{1}=f\left(x_{1}\right)$ | $y_{2}=f\left(x_{2}\right)$ | $\cdots \cdots$ | $y_{n}=f\left(x_{n}\right)$ | $y_{n+1}=f$
$\left(x_{n+1}\right)$ | $\cdots \cdot \cdot$ | Step 2: Plot these points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right),\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right), \ldots \ldots \ldots .,\left(x_{n}, y_{n}\right), \ldots \ldots \ldots$ on rectangular coordinate system. In plotting these points you may use different scales on the $x$ and $y$-axis. Step 3 : Draw a free hand smooth curve passing through the points plotted in step 2 to get the graph of the polynomial $f(x)$. ## GRAPH OF A LINEAR POLYNOMIAL Consider a linear polynomial $f(x)=a x+b, a \neq 0$. In class IX we have studied that the graph of $y=a x+b$ is a straight line that has exactly one zero. That is why $f(x)=a x+b$ is called a linear polynomial. Since two points determine a straight line, so only two points needed to plot or draw the line $y=a x+b$. The line represented by $y=a x+b$ crosses the x -axis at exactly one point, namely $\left(-\frac{b}{a}, 0\right)$.

## GRAPH OF A QUADRATIC POLYNOMIAL

Let $a, b, c$ be real numbers and $a \neq 0$. Then $f(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ is known as quadratic polynomial in $x$ and it has either no zero or two zeroes.

## Example :

1. There are no zeroes of quadratic polynomial $f(x)=x^{2}+1$ and thus the graph of the polynomial doesn't intersect $x$-axis.
2. There are two zeroes of quadratic polynomial $p(x)=x^{2}-4 x+4$ i.e., 2,2 ; which means both zeroes are same and thus the graph of this polynomial intersects $x$-axis only once at 2 .
3. There are two zeroes of quadratic polynomial $g(x)=x^{2}-9$ that is $+3,-3$, thus the graph of this polynomial intersect $x$-axis at two places, $3 \&-3$
Note : (i) The two zeroes of quadratic polynomial can be either both rational or both irrational.
(ii) Graph of a quadratic polynomial is always a parabola.

The graph shown in the illustration 5 and 6 are the parabola.
Some parabola are upward open as in illustration 5 and others are downward open as in illustration 6.
If the value of $a$ in the quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c$ is positive, then the parabola is upward open. If the value of $a$ in the quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c$ is negative, then the parabola is downward open.

Cubic polynomial has either all roots are rational or one is rational and two are irrational. Irrational roots always occur in conjugate pairs.

## RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN ZERO(ES) AND COEFFICIENT OF A POLYNOMIAL

(i) Zero of a linear polynomial $a x+b$, is $x=-\frac{b}{a}$ or $x=-\frac{\text { constant term }}{\operatorname{coefficient} \text { of } x}$
(ii) If quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c=k(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)$, where $k$ is any real constant; then $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are zeroes of quadratic polynomial $a x^{2}+b x+c$, where $a, b, c \in R$ and $a \neq 0$.
$\alpha+\beta=-\frac{b}{a}$, i.e., sum of zeroes $=-\frac{\text { coefficient of } x}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$ and $\alpha . \beta=-\frac{c}{a}$ i.e., product of zeroes $=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$
We can obtain the quadratic polynomial $p(x)$ as $p(x)=k\left[x^{2}-\right.$ (sum of zeroes) $x+$ (product of zeroes)]
(iii) If cubic polynomial $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d=k(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)(x-\gamma)$, where $k$ is any real constant; then $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$ are zeroes of cubic polynomial $a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ where $a, b, c, d \in \mathrm{R}$ and $\mathrm{a} \neq 0$, then
$\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\frac{b}{a}, \quad \alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\frac{c}{a} \quad$ and $\alpha \beta \gamma=-\frac{d}{a}$

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 8

If zeroes of the polynomial $x^{2}+4 x+2 a$ are $\alpha$ and $\frac{2}{\alpha}$, then find the value of $\alpha$.


Given, $\alpha$ and - are the zeroes of $x^{2}+4 x+2 a$.
Now, product of zeroes $=\frac{\text { constant term }}{\text { coefficient of } x^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha \times \frac{2}{\alpha}=2 a \Rightarrow 2=2 \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha=1$

## Some useful formulas :

(i) $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2 \alpha \beta$
(ii) $\alpha^{2}-\beta^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)(\alpha-\beta)=\alpha+\beta \sqrt{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta}$
(iii) $(\alpha-\beta)^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta$
(iv) $\alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=(\alpha+\beta)^{3}-3 \alpha \beta(\alpha+\beta)$
(v) $\alpha^{3}-\beta^{3}=(\alpha-\beta)^{3}+3 \alpha \beta(\alpha-\beta)$

## DIVISION OF POLYNOMIALS

On dividing a polynomial $p(x)$ by a polynomial $d(x)$, let the quotient be $q(x)$ and the remainder be $r(x)$, then $p(x)=d(x) . q(x)+r(x)$, where either $r(x)=0$ or deg. $r(x)<\operatorname{deg} . d(x)$
Here, Dividend $=p(x)$, Divisor $=d(x)$, Quotient $=q(x)$ and Remainder $=r(x)$.
Division algorithm of a polynomial by a polynomial :
Step 1: Arrange the terms of the dividend and the divisor in descending order of their degrees.
Step 2 : Divide the first term of the dividend by the first term of the divisor to obtain the first term of the quotient.
Step 3 : Multiply all the terms of the divisor by the first term of the quotient and subtract the result from the dividend.
Step 4 : Consider the remainder as new dividend and proceed as before.
Step 5 : Repeat this process till we obtain a remainder which is either 0 or a polynomial of degree less than that of the divisor.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-035.jpg?height=61&width=118&top_left_y=1468&top_left_x=1018)

ILLUSTRATION : 10
examples.problem: Find the remainder obtained on dividing $x^{3}+3$ by $x+1$.
examples.solution:
$$
\begin{gathered}
x + 1 \longdiv { x ^ { 3 } + 3 } ( x ^ { 2 } - x + 1 \\
x^{3}+x^{2} \\
\frac{--}{-x^{2}+3} \\
\frac{-x^{2}-x}{+\quad+} \\
\frac{x+3}{x+1} \\
\frac{--}{2}
\end{gathered}
$$

Hence, remainder $=2$
Alternate Method : To find remainder we can use remainder theorem.
Let $f(x)=x^{3}+3$
Put Divisor $=0$
$\Rightarrow x+1=0 \quad \Rightarrow x=-1$
Then remainder $=f(-1)=(-1)^{3}+3=-1+3=2$
So, 2 is the remainder.

examples.problem: Find all the zero(es) of $\mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{4}}-\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}-\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}+\mathbf{6} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{2}$, if you know that two of its zero(es) are $\sqrt{\mathbf{2}}$ and $-\sqrt{\mathbf{2}}$.
examples.solution:
Two zeroes are $\sqrt{2}$ and $-\sqrt{2}$. Hence, $(x-\sqrt{2})$ and $(x+\sqrt{2})$ are the factors of given polynomial.
$\Rightarrow(x-\sqrt{2})(x+\sqrt{2})=x^{2}-2$, is a factor of the given polynomial.
Now, we divide the given polynomial by $x^{2}-2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \begin{aligned}
x^{2}-2 \sqrt{\frac{2 x^{2}-3 x+1}{2 x^{4}-3 x^{3}-3 x^{2}+6 x-2}-4 x^{2}} & \\
\frac{-3 x^{3}+x^{2}+6 x-2}{-3 x^{3}} & \quad \text { (First term of quotient is } \frac{2 x^{4}}{x^{2}}=2 x^{2} \text { ) }
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \begin{array}{ll}
x^{2} & -2 \\
- & +
\end{array} \text { (Third term of quotient is } \frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}}=1 \text { ) }
\end{aligned}
$$

So, $2 x^{4}-3 x^{3}-3 x^{2}+6 x-2=\left(x^{2}-2\right)\left(2 x^{2}-3 x+1\right)$.
Now, by splitting $-3 x$, we factorise $2 x^{2}-3 x+1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 x^{2}-3 x+1 & =2 x^{2}-2 x-x+1 \\
& =2 x(x-1)-1(x-1) \\
& =(x-1)(2 x-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

So, the remaining two zero(es) are given by $x=\frac{1}{2}$ and $x=1$. Therefore, the zero(es) of the given polynomial are $\sqrt{2},-\sqrt{2}, \frac{1}{2}$ and 1 .

# CASE STUDY : Division Algorithm, Factor Theorem, Remainder Theorem and zero(es) of the Polynomial 

- Division Algorithm :
$($ Dividend $)=($ Quotient $) \times($ Divisor $)+($ Remainder $)$
- Factor Theorem :
$(x-a)$ is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f(a)=0$
$(x+a)$ is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f(-a)=0$
$(a x-b)$ is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)=0$
- Remainder Theorem :
$(a x+b)$ is a factor of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right)=0$
If polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(x-a)$, then remainder $=f(a)$
If polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(x+a)$, then remainder $=f(-a)$
If polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(a x-b)$, then remainder $=f\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$
- Zeroes of a Polynomial

If polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(a x+b)$, then remainder $=f\left(-\frac{b}{a}\right)$
Zeroes of a Polynomial

A number $\alpha$ is a zero of polynomial $f(x)$, if $f(\alpha)=0$.
Number of zero(s) of a polynomial $=$ Degree of the polynomial.
Hence number zero(s) of alinear polynomial, quadratic polynomial, cubic polynomial and biquadratic polynomial are $1,2,3$ and 4 respectively.

CASE-II : To find the quotient and remainder using division algorithm
Illustration: Apply the division algorithm to find the quotient and remainder on dividing $f(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}+11 x-6$, by $g(x)=x+2$.

Sol. We have,
$f(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}+11 x-6$ and $g(x)=x+2$
We find that degree $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=3$ and degree $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=1$. Therefore, quotient $\mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x})$ is of degree $3-1=2$ and the remainder $\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x})$ is of degree zero.

Let $\mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{ax}^{2}+\mathrm{bx}+\mathrm{c}$ and $\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{k}$.
By using division algorithm, we obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) \times \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{x}) \\
\Rightarrow \quad & x^{3}-6 x^{2}+11 x-6=\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)(x+2)+k \\
\Rightarrow \quad & x^{3}-6 x^{2}+11 x-6=a x^{3}+(2 a+b) x^{2}+(2 b+c) x+(2 c+k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating the coefficients of like powers of $x$ and can start term on both sides, we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
1=a & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{3}\right] } \\
-6=2 a+b & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{2}\right] } \\
11=2 b+c & {[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x] } \\
\text { and },-6=2 c+k & {[\text { On equating the coefficients terms }] }
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving these equations, we get $a=1, b=-8, c=27$ and $k=-60$
$\therefore \quad$ Quotient $q(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c=x^{2}-8 x+27$ and, Remainder $r(x)=k=-60$.

CASE-I : To find the remaining zeroes of a polynomial when some of its zeroes are given

Illustration: Obtain all the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)=3 x^{4}+6 x^{3}-2 x^{2} -10 x-5$, if two of its zeroes are $\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ and $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$.

Sol. It is given that $\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ and $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}$ are two zeroes of $f(x)$. Therefore, $\left(x-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right)\left(x+\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right)$ is a factor of $f(x)$. But, $\left(x-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right)\left(x+\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}\right) =\left(x^{2}-\frac{5}{3}\right)=\frac{1}{3}\left(3 x^{2}-5\right)$. Therefore, $\left(3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-5\right)$ is a factor of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$.

Let us now divide $f(x)$ by $3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-5$. Using long division method, we obtain

$$
\begin{gathered}
3 x ^ { 2 } - 5 \longdiv { 3 x ^ { 4 } + 6 x ^ { 3 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 0 x - 5 } x ^ { 2 } + 2 x + 1 \\
\frac{3 x^{4}-5 x^{2}}{-\quad+} \\
\begin{array}{c}
6 x^{3}+3 x^{2}-10 x-5 \\
\frac{6 x^{3}-10 x}{+} \\
\frac{3 x^{2}-5}{3 x^{2}-5} \\
\frac{-+}{0}
\end{array}
\end{gathered}
$$

Clearly, Quotient $=x^{2}+2 x+1$ and Remainder $=0$.

By division algorithm, we obtain
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left(3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-5\right)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{x}+1\right)+0$
$\Rightarrow f(x)=(\sqrt{3} x+\sqrt{5})(\sqrt{3} x-\sqrt{5})(x+1)^{2}$
Thus, the factors of $f(x)$ are $\sqrt{3} x+\sqrt{5}, \sqrt{3} x-\sqrt{5}, x+1$ and $x+1$.
Equating each factor to zero, we obtain $x=-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}, \sqrt{\frac{5}{3}},-1,-1$. Hence,
the zeroes of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ are $-\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}, \sqrt{\frac{5}{3}},-1$ and -1 .

## CASE-III : To check whether a given polynomial in a factor of the other polynomial by applying the Division Algorithm

Illustration: By applying division algorithm prove that the polynomial $g(x)=x^{2}+3 x+1$ is a factor of the polynomial $f(x)=3 x^{4}+5 x^{3}-7 x^{2}+2 x+2$
Sol. We find that degree of $f(x)=4$ and degree $g(x)=2$. Therefore, quotient $q(x)$ is of degree $2(=4-2)$ and the remainder $r(x)$ is of degree 1 or less. Let $q(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ and $r(x)=p x+q$.
Using division algorithm, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=g(x) \times q(x)+r(x) \\
\Rightarrow & 3 x^{4}+5 x^{3}-7 x^{2}+2 x+2=\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)\left(x^{2}+3 x+1\right)+(p x+q) \\
\Rightarrow & 3 x^{4}+5 x^{3}-7 x^{2}+2 x+2=a x^{4}+(3 a+b) x^{3}+(a+3 b+c) x^{2}+(b+3 c+p) x+(c+q)
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating coefficients of various powers of $x$ and constant term from both sides, we get

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
a=3 & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{4}\right]} \\
3 a+b=5 & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{3}\right]} \\
a+c+3 b=-7 & {\left[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x^{2}\right]} \\
b+3 c+p=2 & {[\text { On equating the coefficients of } x]} \\
c+q=2 & {[\text { On equating the constant terms }]}
\end{array}
$$

Solving these equations, we get

$$
a=3, b=-4, c=2, p=0 \text { and } q=0
$$

∴ Quotient $q(x)=3 x^{2}-4 x+2$ and, Remainder $r(x)=0 x+0=0$
Since, remainder $r(x)=0$. Hence, $g(x)$ is a factor of $f(x)$.

## Think Out of the Box

Q. 1. If $x=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}$ is a root of the polynomial $f(x)=2 k x^{2}-7 x+k$, then find other root of the polynomial $f(x)$.
[Hint: $f\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}\right)=0 \Rightarrow k=\sqrt{3} . \quad$ Ans. $x=\sqrt{3}$ ]
Q. 2. If the quotient obtained on dividing the polynomial $f(x)=3 x^{3}+11 x^{2}+34 x+106$ by $g(x)=x-3$ using division algorithm is $3 x^{2}+p x+q$, then find $p$, $q$ and also the reminder.
[Hint: $f(x)=g(x)\left(3 x^{2}+20 x+94\right)+388$.
Ans. $p=20, q=94$, remainder $=388$ ]

## MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES

1. $f(x)=x^{2}-2 x-3$. Find the roots of $f(x)$, and sketch the graph of $y=f(x)$.

Sol. $f(x)=x^{2}-2 x-3=(x+1)(x-3)$. Therefore, the roots are -1 and 3 .
The graph intersects the X -axis at $x=-1$ and 3 .

2. How many real roots, i.e., roots that are real numbers, has the parabola of each graph ?
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=337&width=339&top_left_y=1356&top_left_x=324)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=337&width=335&top_left_y=1356&top_left_x=687)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=357&width=332&top_left_y=1336&top_left_x=1037)

Sol. Graph (a) has two real roots. It has two x -intercepts (i.e., the graph intersects the X -axis at two different points)
Graph (b) has no real roots. It has no x-intercepts. Both roots are complex.
Graph (c) has two real roots which are equal.
3. Inspect the following graph to solve this equation : $\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{5}=\mathbf{0}$

Sol. The quadratic polynomial will have the value 0 at $x=-1$ and 5 .
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/9afb5168-3a1c-43f6-9248-79825029b395-039.jpg?height=613&width=684&top_left_y=1941&top_left_x=719)
4. Find the zero(es) of the polynomial $\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{- 3}$ and verify the relationship between the zero(es) and the coefficients.

Sol. Recall the identity $a^{2}-b^{2}=(a-b)(a+b)$. Using it, we can write:
$x^{2}-3=(x-\sqrt{3})(x+\sqrt{3})$
So, the value of $x^{2}-3$ is zero when $x=\sqrt{3}$ or $x=-\sqrt{3}$
Therefore, the zeroes of $x^{2}-3$ are $\sqrt{3}$ and $-\sqrt{3}$

Now, Sum of zeroes $=\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{3}=0=\frac{-(\text { Coefficient of } x)}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}$

$$
\text { Product of zeroes }=(\sqrt{3})(-\sqrt{3})=-3=\frac{-3}{1}=\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}
$$

5. Show that $\boldsymbol{x}=\mathbf{2}$ is a root of $\mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}+\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{7} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{6}$

Sol. Let $p(x)=2 x^{3}+x^{2}-7 x-6$
$p(2)=2(2)^{3}+(2)^{2}-7(2)-6=16+4-14-6=0$
Hence $x=2$ is a root of $p(x)$.
6. If $\boldsymbol{x}=\frac{\mathbf{4}}{\mathbf{3}}$ is a root of the polynomial $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})=\mathbf{6} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}-\mathbf{1 1} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}+\boldsymbol{k} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{2 0}$, then find the value of $\boldsymbol{k}$.

Sol. Let $f(x)=6 x^{3}-11 x^{2}+k x-20$ and if $x=\frac{4}{3}$ is root of polynomial, then $f\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)=0$
$\therefore f\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)=6\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{3}-11\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{2}+k\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)-20=0$
$\Rightarrow 6\left(\frac{64}{27}\right)-11\left(\frac{16}{9}\right)+\frac{4 k}{3}-20=0 \Rightarrow 128-176+12 k-180=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 12 k+128-356=0 \Rightarrow 12 k=228 \Rightarrow k=19$
7. Find the remainder when $f(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}+2 x-4$ is divided by $g(x)=1-2 x$.

Sol. Put $1-2 x=0 \Rightarrow 2 x=1 \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}$
Remainder $=f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3}-6\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)-4=\frac{1}{8}-\frac{3}{2}+1-4=\frac{1-12+8-32}{8}=-\frac{35}{8}$
8. The polynomials $a x^{3}+3 x^{2}-13$ and $2 x^{3}-5 x+a$ are divided by $x+2$ if the remainder in each case is the same, find the value of $\boldsymbol{a}$.
Sol. Let $p(x)=a x^{3}+3 x^{2}-13$ and $q(x)=2 x^{3}-5 x+a$
When $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ are divided by $x+2=0 \Rightarrow x=-2$
$\therefore p(-2)=q(-2) \Rightarrow a(-2)^{3}+3(-2)^{2}-13=2(-2)^{3}-5(-2)+a \Rightarrow-8 a+12-13=-16+10+a$
$\Rightarrow-9 a=-5 \Rightarrow a=\frac{5}{9}$
9. Find $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ and $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ if $\boldsymbol{x}+\mathbf{1}$ and $\boldsymbol{x}+\mathbf{2}$ are factors of $\boldsymbol{p}(\boldsymbol{x})=\boldsymbol{x}^{3}+3 \boldsymbol{x}^{2}-2 \alpha \boldsymbol{x}+\beta$

Sol. Put $x+1=0$ or $x=-1$ and $x+2=0$ or $x=-2$ in $p(x)$
Then, $p(-1)=0$ and $p(-2)=0$

$$
\Rightarrow p(-1)=(-1)^{3}+3(-1)^{2}-2 \alpha(-1)+\beta=0 \Rightarrow-1+3+2 \alpha+\beta=0 \Rightarrow \beta=-2 \alpha-2
$$

$$
p(-2)=(-2)^{3}+3(-2)^{2}-2 \alpha(-2)+\beta=0 \quad \Rightarrow-8+12+4 \alpha+\beta=0 \Rightarrow \beta=-4 \alpha-4
$$

By equalising equations (i) and (ii), we get
$-2 \alpha-2=-4 \alpha-4 \Rightarrow 2 \alpha=-2 \Rightarrow \alpha=-1$
put $\alpha=-1$ in eq. (i) $\Rightarrow \beta=-2(-1)-2=2-2=0$ Hence, $\alpha=-1, \beta=0$
10. What must be added to $\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}+\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{2} \mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{x}+\mathbf{9}$, so that the result is exactly divisible by $\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}+\mathbf{7} \boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{6}$.

Sol. Let $p(x)=3 x^{3}+x^{2}-22 x+9$ and $r(x)=3 x^{2}+7 x-6$
We know that if $p(x)$ is not exactly divisible by $q(x)$, then the remainder be $r(x)$ and degree of $r(x)$ is less than $q(x)$ or divisor.

Let, we add $a x+b$ (linear polynomial) in $p(x)$, so that $p(x)+a x+b$ is exactly divisible by $3 x^{2}+7 x-6$
Hence, $\quad p(x)+a x+b=s(x)=3 x^{3}+x^{2}-22 x+9+a x+b$

$$
\begin{gathered}
=3 x^{3}+x^{2}-x(22-a)+(9+b) \\
3 x ^ { 2 } + 7 x - 6 \longdiv { 3 x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } - x ( 2 2 - a ) + 9 + b ( x - 2 } \\
\begin{array}{l}
3 x^{3}+7 x^{2}-6 x \\
-\quad+\quad+ \\
-6 x^{2}+6 x-(22-a) x+9+b
\end{array} \\
\text { or } \\
\begin{array}{l}
-6 x^{2}+x(-16+a)+9+b \\
-6 x^{2}-14 x+12 \\
+\quad- \\
x(-2+a)+(b-3)=0
\end{array}
\end{gathered}
$$

Hence, $\quad x(a-2)+b-3=0 . x+0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a-2=0$ and $b-3=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=2$ or $b=3$
Hence, if in $p(x)$ we add $a x+b$ or $2 x+3$ then it is exactly divisible by $3 x^{2}+7 x-6$.

## Alternate Method :

Let $p(x)=3 x^{3}+\mathrm{x}^{2}-22 x+9$
and $\mathrm{r}(x)=3 x^{2}+7 x-6$
Let $q$ be added in polynomial $p(x)$, so that it will be exactly divisible by $r(x)$
Clearly the degree of $q$ will be less than $r(x)$.
So, $p(x)+q=3 x^{3+} x^{2}-22 x+9+q$

$$
\begin{gathered}
3 x ^ { 2 } + 7 x - 6 \longdiv { 3 x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } - 2 2 x + 9 + q } ( x - 2 \\
\frac{3 x^{3}+7 x^{2}-6 x}{-\quad+} \\
\frac{-6 x^{2}-16 x+9}{-6 x^{2}-14 x+12} \\
\frac{+\quad+\quad}{-2 x-3+q}
\end{gathered}
$$

Since, $p(x)+q$ is exactly divisible by $r(x)$, then remainder must be zero.
$\therefore-2 x-3+q=0 \Rightarrow q=2 x+3$
Hence, $2 x+3$ must be added to make polynomial exactly divisible by $3 x^{2}+7 x-6$.
11. What must be subtracted from $\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{3}}-\mathbf{6} \boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{1 5} \boldsymbol{x}+\mathbf{8 0}$, so that the result is exactly divisible by $\boldsymbol{x}^{\mathbf{2}}+\boldsymbol{x}-\mathbf{1 2}$.

Sol. In above problem, we see that if in $p(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}-15 x+80$ we subtracted $a x+b$ so that is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+x-12$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q(x) & =x^{3}-6 x^{2}-15 x+80-(a x+b) \\
& =x^{3}-6 x^{2}-(15+a) x+(80-b)
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividend $=$ Divisor × quotient + remainder
But remainder will be zero.
∴ Dividend $=$ Divisor × quotient
$q(x)=\left(x^{2}+x-12\right) \times$ quotient
$q(x)=x^{3}-6 x^{2}-(15+a) x+(80-b)=x\left(x^{2}+x-12\right)-7\left(x^{2}+x-12\right)=x^{3}+x^{2}-7 x^{2}-12 x-7 x+84=x^{3}-6 x^{2}-19 x+84$
Hence, $x^{3}-6 x^{2}-19 x+84=x^{3}-6 x^{2}-(15+a) x+(80-b)$
$-15-a=-19 \Rightarrow a=+4$ and $80-b=84 \Rightarrow b=-4$
Hence, if in $p(x)$ we subtracted $4 x-4=(a x+b)$, then it is exactly divisible by $x^{2}+x-12$
Alternate Method :
Factors of $x^{2}+x-12=(x+4)(x-3)$ Put $x^{2}+x-12=0 \Rightarrow(x+4)(x-3)=0$
then either $x=-4$ or $3 q(-4)=(-4)^{3}-6(-4)^{2}-(15+a)(-4)+(80-b) \Rightarrow 4 a-b=20$
and $q(3)=(3)^{3}-6(3)^{2}-(15+a)(3)+80-b \Rightarrow 3 a+b=8$

Solving equations (i) \& (ii), we get

$$
a=4 \& \mathrm{~b}=-4
$$

$\therefore \quad a x+b=4 x-4$, must be subtracted

## Multiple Choice Questions

DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which only one is correct.

1. If the zeroes of the polynomial $f(x)=k^{2} x^{2}-17 x+k+2$, $(k>0)$ are reciprocal of each other than value of $k$ is

(b) -1
(c) -2
(d) 1

(d) $\frac{-15}{2}$

(b) 3
(c) -5
(d) 5

(b) 1
(c) $\frac{1}{2}$

(b) 14
(c) 21
(d) 6

9. If $a$ and $b$ are zeroes of the polynomial
$2 t^{2}-4 t+3$, then the value of $a^{2} b+a b^{2}$ is :

10. The zeroes of the polynomial $x^{2}-3 x-m(m+3)$ are

2. The zeroes of a polynomial $p(x)$ are precisely the $x$-coordinates of the points, where the graph of $y=p(x)$ intersects the $\_\_\_\_$ -axis.

## True / False

DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false.

1. For polynomials $p(x)$ and any non-zero polynomial $g(x)$, there are polynomials $q(x)$ and $r(x)$ such that $p(x)=g(x) q(x)+r(x)$, where $r(x)=0$ or degree $r(x)<$ degree $g(x)$.
2. Sum of zeroes of quadratic polynomial $=-\frac{(\text { coefficient of } x)}{\left(\text { coefficient of } x^{2}\right)}$
3. $\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} x^{\frac{1}{2}}+1$ is a polynomial
4. $\frac{6 \sqrt{x}+x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\sqrt{x}}$ is a polynomial, $x \neq 0$
5. Product of zeroes of quadratic polynomial
$=-\frac{\text { constant term }}{\left(\text { coefficient of } x^{2}\right)}$
6. A polynomial cannot have more than one zero
7. The degree of the sum of two polynomials each of degree 5 is always 5 .
8. $3,-1,1 / 3$ are the zeroes of the cubic polynomial
$p(x)=3 x^{3}-5 x^{2}-11 x-3$.
9. Zeroes of quadratic polynomial $x^{2}+7 x+10$ are 2 and -5
10. Sum of zeroes of $2 x^{2}-8 x+6$ is -4

## Match the Following

DIRECTIONS : Each question contains statements given in two columns which have to be matched. Statements ( $A, B, C, D$ ) in column-I have to be matched with statements ( $p, q, r, s$ ) in column-II.
1.

Column-I (Polynomial)

Column-II (Remainder)
(A) $\frac{x^{3}-3 x^{2}+x+2}{x^{2}-x+1}$ (p) 8
(B) $\frac{x^{3}-3 x^{2}+5 x-3}{x+2}$ (q) $\quad x-5$
(C) $\frac{x^{4}-6 x^{3}+16 x^{2}-25 x+10}{x^{2}-2 x+5}$ (r) -33
(D) $\frac{x^{4}-3 x^{2}+4 x+5}{x^{2}-x+1}$
(s) $-2 x+4$

## Very Short Answer Questions

1. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the zeroes of the quadratic polynomial $f(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ then evaluate $\frac{1}{\alpha^{3}}+\frac{1}{\beta^{3}}$.
2. Find a quadratic polynomial, the sum and product of whose zeroes are -3 and 2 , respectively.
3. Let $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ be the roots of $2 x^{3}-3 x^{2}+6 x+1$. Find the value of $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha$.
4. For some polynomials $p(x)$ the graph of $y=p(x)$ are given below. Find the number of zeroes of $p(x)$ in each case.

5. The graph of $y=p(x)$ are given in fig. below for some polynomials $p(x)$. Find the number of zeroes of $p(x)$ in each case.
